comment: This is a report on a press conference where ISKCON and allied Sanatan organizations deny accusations of being militant/extremist, presenting their self-description as peaceful and humanitarian. The article functions as a neutral relay of their press statement and grievances (alleged harassment, threats) without independent editorial endorsement or criticism from the reporter.

This bylined opinion/analysis piece on West Bengal politics frames ISKCON (via Radharaman Das) as an active instrument in a political propaganda apparatus, describing them as a major 'producer' of Bangladesh-centered propaganda that both BJP and Mamata Banerjee exploit for electoral gain. This is the author's own analytical framing, not merely quoted rhetoric, casting ISKCON's local leadership as politically self-interested and instrumentalized rather than neutral religious actors, giving it a mildly critical cast.

This opinion piece critiques Indian media's narrative around Chinmoy Krishna Das's arrest rather than ISKCON itself. It notes ISKCON's global reputation for humanitarian work (food distribution, schools, disaster relief) neutrally-to-positively, while also factually noting ISKCON expelled Chinmoy before his arrest and cautioning that religious leadership doesn't preclude wrongdoing (illustrated via an unrelated Buddhist monk example, not ISKCON). The framing toward ISKCON itself remains balanced and non-judgmental.

A neutral fact-check article debunking a viral claim that a video showed an attack on an ISKCON cow farm in Bangladesh, establishing the footage was actually from Punjab, India. The piece is procedural and evidence-based, focused on verifying the video's origin rather than framing ISKCON positively or negatively.

A factual report on a Hefazat-led human chain demanding a ban on ISKCON following a lawyer's killing. The demand and characterization of a 'group' destabilizing the country are quoted from Hefazat speakers; the reporter's own voice remains a neutral conduit without adopting the accusatory framing.

This reports a Hefazat rally in Chittagong demanding ISKCON's ban and execution of a lawyer's killers, including a leader's direct quote calling ISKCON an 'extremist organization' and setting conditions for dialogue. All strongly negative characterizations are attributed quotations from Hefazat leaders; the journalistic framing itself is a neutral, procedural account of the rally and demands.

The article reports a Hefazat rally where leader Mamunul Hoque labels ISKCON a 'terrorist-militant organization' linked to RAW and Sheikh Hasina, demanding its ban. This extreme rhetoric is entirely attributed to quoted speakers; the reporter maintains a neutral, descriptive stance covering the event without independently endorsing or condemning the characterization of ISKCON.

A purely factual, administrative report on Bangladesh Bank's financial intelligence unit suspending bank transactions for Chinmoy Krishna Das and 16 ISKCON-linked individuals under anti-money-laundering law. The piece lists procedural details without any evaluative language toward ISKCON.
